mission.title: Install and Configure Vitess on Kubernetes
mission.description: Vitess is a cloud-native horizontally-scalable distributed database system built around MySQL, enabling unlimited scaling through generalized sharding. Installing Vitess allows you to manage large-scale databases efficiently and maintain application performance as your data grows.

steps.title: Add the Vitess Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Vitess Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add vitess https://vitess.io/helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Vitess using Helm
steps.description:
Install Vitess in a new namespace called 'vitess' using the following command:
```bash
helm install vitess vitess/vitess --namespace vitess --create-namespace --version v23.0.3
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Vitess pods are running successfully with the command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace vitess
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Vitess deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to open the deployment configuration:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment vitess --namespace vitess
```
In the editor, add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the Vitess service to access it:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/vitess 15999:15999 --namespace vitess
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Vitess will have all pods running in the 'vitess' namespace, and you will be able to access the Vitess service through the forwarded port. You can verify the resource limits by checking the deployment configuration.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add vitess https://vitess.io/helm
helm repo update
helm install vitess vitess/vitess --namespace vitess --create-namespace --version v23.0.3

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Vitess release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall vitess --namespace vitess
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. This ensures that no leftover resources remain.
```bash
kubectl delete crd vitessclusters.vitess.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace vitess
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'vitess' namespace to clean up all resources associated with the installation. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace vitess
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of the Vitess installation. You can use the following command to export the current configuration.
```bash
helm get values vitess --namespace vitess > vitess-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Vitess installation to the latest version. Replace 'v23.0.4' with the desired version if necessary.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade vitess vitess/vitess --namespace vitess --version v23.0.4
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check the status of the Vitess pods to ensure they are running the new version without issues.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace vitess
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Vitess pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors that may indicate what is causing the crash.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace vitess
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Vitess service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly and that the service is running. Check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace vitess
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources allocated
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, verify the resource limits set in the deployment. You can check the current resource allocation with:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment vitess --namespace vitess
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, ensure that you are using a compatible version of the Vitess chart. Check the available versions with:
```bash
helm search repo vitess --namespace vitess
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
